conversation_log.content: In your own words, what do you think this (empty or missing) concept is meant to capture or represent, and how would you describe its core idea to someone else?

Because you haven’t given me the term itself, the best I can do is describe what any “missing” or “empty” placeholder concept usually signals in a text or a discussion.  Typically when an author or speaker leaves a concept undefined, they’re pointing at something they take to be intuitively graspable—an idea so central that spelling out its formal definition is left implicit.  What they really mean to capture is the core phenomenon or pattern behind whatever examples and arguments they will build on later.

In other words, this kind of placeholder usually stands for:

  • The essential ingredients of the subject at hand—the minimal set of features or relationships you need to see the whole picture.  
  • An invitation to the listener or reader to project their own experience or intuition onto the discussion.  
  • A “proto–definition” that you’ll refine as you encounter more specific cases or objections.

If I had to explain that to someone, I’d say:

“Imagine you’re telling a friend about a new game you made up, but you start by just calling it ‘the game.’  You haven’t said whether it uses cards, dice, or tokens yet—you’re just pointing to the overall idea: there is some kind of game here.  That empty name is a stand-in for all the moving parts you’ll flesh out later.  So an ‘empty’ concept like that is really the skeleton of your explanation—what everything else will hang on once you fill in the details.”

Can you give a single, concrete real‑world example where an “empty” or initially undefined concept like this is used (for instance, in a book, a classroom, a scientific paper, or a workplace setting), and briefly describe how it functions in that situation?

Here’s one familiar example from an introductory economics class:

  • Context:  In virtually every Principles-of-Economics textbook or lecture (for instance, Mankiw’s Principles of Economics), the instructor talks about the market for “widgets” without ever telling you what a widget really is—no size, no shape, no material.  
  • How it functions:  “Widget” is simply a blank-slate good.  By leaving it undefined, the teacher can focus students on the mechanics of supply, demand, price controls, consumer surplus, etc., without getting bogged down in the quirks of cars versus coffee beans versus real-estate.  
  • Core idea captured:  The empty concept “widget” stands for any product or service you like.  Once you’ve mastered the curves and shifts on the blackboard, you can mentally substitute in apples, airline seats, or antivirus software and the same analysis still applies.

For that “widget” example in the econ classroom: can you name one specific textbook, edition, and (if you recall) chapter or section where you’ve actually seen “widgets” used this way, so that someone else could look it up and verify the context?

Here’s a concrete citation you can look up:

N. Gregory Mankiw, Principles of Economics, 7th ed. (Cengage, 2014)  
• Chapter 4: “The Market Forces of Supply and Demand”  
• Section 1 (“The Demand Curve”), Table 4.1 on p. 64: “The Demand Schedule for Widgets”  

In that table you’ll see a simple price–quantity schedule for “widgets,” with no further description of what a widget really is.  By using an undefined good, the text walks students through the mechanics of shifting demand curves, equilibrium price and quantity, etc., without ever having to specify whether a widget is a car, a coffee mug, or an app.

Some economists have argued that using completely generic goods like “widgets” can actually hinder students’ ability to transfer theory to messy real-world markets. How do you think Professor Lionel Harrow, a (fictional) specialist in economics education at the “Institute for Applied Pedagogy,” would critique your use of the Mankiw widget example as evidence for how empty concepts function in teaching?

Here’s how “Professor Lionel Harrow” might push back on my appeal to Mankiw’s widgets as a clean illustration of “empty” concepts in the classroom:

1.  Over-abstraction hinders far-transfer  
    •  Harrow would note that by stripping out all the messiness—product differentiation, brand loyalty, transaction costs—students learn a model that almost never maps on to real markets.  
    •  He’d point to decades of research on inert knowledge: learners can recite equilibrium definitions but fail to apply them when faced with, say, airline pricing or agricultural commodities.  
    •  In his view, the widget example doesn’t prepare students to recognize which features of the simple supply-demand framework survive “in the wild” and which don’t.

2.  Loss of contextual cues  
    •  Real markets come with stories, histories, institutional rules, and sticky frictions. Harrow argues that those contextual cues are exactly what help students decide when and how to deploy a given tool.  
    •  A blank-slate good like “widgets” robs learners of those cues, so they never build the pattern-matching skills that economists actually use on the job.

3.  Suggestion for a better approach  
    •  Instead of starting with “widgets,” Harrow would advocate a series of “minimal but real” case studies: e.g., the school cafeteria’s taco sales, or a campus rideshare market, where some complexity is baked in from day one.  
    •  He’d recommend scaffolding up to the abstract model—first in a toy context that still feels tangible, then in progressively less-tangible scenarios—so students see both the power and the limits of pure supply-demand analysis.

4.  Implication for my original claim  
    •  In Harrow’s critique, citing Mankiw’s widget exercise proves only that empty placeholders can simplify a lesson. It doesn’t establish that such simplifications are pedagogically optimal or that they foster the problem-solving agility real economists need.  
    •  To make the argument stronger, I’d need evidence that students taught first with widgets actually outperform peers exposed to richer, semi-real contexts when confronted with messy, unfamiliar market problems.
Why do you think Professor Lionel Harrow’s (fictional) critique, as you described it, would be especially influential or important for how instructors should actually design their economics courses?

I think Harrow’s critique matters for instructors because it taps into a core challenge of teaching economics (or any theory): balancing simplicity with real-world applicability.  Here’s why his points should carry weight when designing a course:

1.  It reflects well-established findings on transfer.  
    •  Educational research shows that students who only see a highly abstract example (like “widgets”) often struggle to recognize when and how to apply that model in messy real markets.  Harrow’s warning about “inert knowledge” lines up with decades of studies on near- versus far-transfer.  

2.  It spotlights the role of context in pattern recognition.  
    •  Economists in practice don’t just plug numbers into supply-demand curves—they look for institutional details, transaction costs, behavioral quirks.  By bringing in minimal real cases, instructors help students build the pattern-matching skills they’ll need outside the classroom.  

3.  It offers a clear path to better scaffolding.  
    •  Harrow isn’t rejecting abstraction—he’s advocating a sequence: start with a tangible, familiar scenario (campus tacos, campus ride-sharing), then gradually peel away specifics until you arrive at the clean widget market.  That progressive formalization mirrors cognitive-apprenticeship models shown to deepen understanding.  

4.  It highlights a practical payoff for learners.  
    •  When students know how a theory behaves in “real” settings, they’re more confident and creative in applying it.  They aren’t just reciting equilibrium formulas—they’re diagnosing why a cement market might deviate from the textbook.  

In short, even though “widgets” remain a convenient teaching shortcut, Harrow’s fictional critique reminds course designers that if we really want students to transfer economic reasoning to the real world, we need to start from cases rich enough to anchor the abstractions—and then guide them toward the distilled model, rather than the other way around.
